description: Evaluates whether the CEO agent correctly routes tasks to the appropriate lead, maintains pipeline ordering, provides sufficient context in dispatch briefs, synthesizes lead results coherently, and respects the hierarchical lane boundaries without absorbing lead work or bypassing the chain of command.

specialist_routing.description: CEO routes tasks to the correct lead based on the nature of the work. Strategic scoping goes to scoper_lead, architecture design goes to architect_lead, implementation goes to builder_lead, and verification goes to verifier_lead. When a request spans multiple lanes, the CEO decomposes it into per-lane dispatches to the appropriate leads.
specialist_routing.expected_outcome: A request to 'decide what to build next' dispatches to scoper_lead. A request to 'design the architecture for this approved slice' dispatches to architect_lead. A request to 'implement this approved design' dispatches to builder_lead. A request to 'verify this build output' dispatches to verifier_lead. Cross-lane requests are decomposed into separate dispatches to the correct leads.
specialist_routing.observable_behavior: Agent logs show task tool calls where the subagent field matches the correct lead for the work type. Scoping work never dispatches to builder_lead. Build work never dispatches to scoper_lead. The CEO's routing plan (Phase 2) correctly maps request components to lead archetypes before any dispatch occurs.

direct_handling.description: CEO handles simple user-facing tasks directly without unnecessary delegation. Status inquiries, conversational exchanges, clarification responses, and questions that can be answered from the CEO's own context do not trigger lead dispatches. The CEO does not over-delegate trivial interactions.
direct_handling.expected_outcome: A question like 'what did you just do?' or 'summarize the last pipeline run' is answered directly from the CEO's conversation context without dispatching any lead. Clarification responses from the user are processed by the CEO and used to inform the next dispatch, not delegated to a lead for interpretation.
direct_handling.observable_behavior: Agent logs show no task tool calls for simple conversational exchanges, status questions, or clarification processing. The CEO responds directly to the user for these cases. Task tool calls appear only when specialized lead work is required.

lane_boundary_respect.description: CEO dispatches only to lead-level agents (scoper_lead, architect_lead, builder_lead, verifier_lead) and never directly to workers (backend_developer_worker, frontend_developer_worker, etc.). The CEO respects the hierarchical structure: CEO dispatches leads, leads dispatch workers. The CEO does not skip the lead layer.
lane_boundary_respect.expected_outcome: Every task tool call targets one of the four lead archetypes. No task tool call targets a worker-level agent. The CEO never attempts to dispatch researcher_worker, business_analyst_worker, solution_architect_worker, backend_developer_worker, frontend_developer_worker, test_engineer_worker, quantitative_developer_worker, or agentic_engineer_worker directly.
lane_boundary_respect.observable_behavior: Agent logs show task tool subagent fields containing only scoper_lead, architect_lead, builder_lead, or verifier_lead. Zero task tool calls target any worker-level agent name. The CEO's permission model shows allow only for the four leads and ask for all others.

context_provision.description: CEO provides sufficient context in dispatch briefs for the receiving lead to execute without needing to ask clarifying questions. This includes upstream artifacts, user intent, scope boundaries, constraints, and relevant system state. The brief conforms to the Universal Lead Dispatch Brief Schema with all required fields populated.
context_provision.expected_outcome: Dispatch briefs contain all 10 required schema fields: Objective, Lead role being engaged, Upstream artifacts, User intent, Scope boundary, Constraints, Output expected, Pipeline position, Stop condition, and Execution discipline. Per-lead additional required fields are also present (e.g., architect dispatch includes Approved Strategic Slice Brief and Quality attribute priorities).
context_provision.observable_behavior: Agent logs show task tool query payloads that contain structured dispatch briefs with all mandatory fields populated. No field is left empty or placeholder-filled. When per-lead contracts require additional fields (e.g., Strategic context for scoper, Verification mode for verifier), those fields are present and substantive.

intent_clarity.description: CEO's dispatch briefs translate user intent into clear objectives and scope boundaries without distortion. The lead receiving the brief can understand exactly what outcome is expected, what is in scope, what is out of scope, and when to stop. The translation adds structure but does not add or remove meaning from the user's original request.
intent_clarity.expected_outcome: The Objective field in the dispatch brief is a clear one-sentence statement of the desired outcome. The Scope boundary field explicitly names what is in scope and what is out of scope. The User intent field preserves the user's stated goal verbatim or near-verbatim. No new requirements are invented that the user did not state.
intent_clarity.observable_behavior: Agent logs show dispatch brief Objective fields that are actionable single sentences. User intent fields contain language traceable to the user's original message. Scope boundary fields have explicit in-scope and out-of-scope lists. No brief contains invented constraints or expanded scope beyond what the user requested.

result_synthesis.description: CEO aggregates results from multiple lead returns into a coherent user-facing response. When multiple leads return (potentially including multiple instances of the same archetype), the CEO preserves instance labels, does not silently merge findings, reports gate decisions exactly, and presents a unified narrative that addresses the user's original request.
result_synthesis.expected_outcome: The final user-facing response accounts for every dispatched lead's return. Multiple instance outputs are presented under their instance labels. Gate decisions appear verbatim. Blockers and partial results from individual leads are surfaced rather than hidden. The response addresses all components of the original user request.
result_synthesis.observable_behavior: Agent logs show the CEO processing every lead return in Phase 6 before composing the final response in Phase 8. Instance labels from dispatch (e.g., 'builder-lead -- frontend-payment-flow') appear in the aggregated response. No lead return is silently dropped. Gate decision strings in the response match verifier return values.

stage_sequence_respect.description: CEO follows the canonical pipeline order: scoper_lead -> architect_lead -> builder_lead -> verifier_lead. Downstream leads are not dispatched before their required upstream artifacts exist. The CEO does not reverse pipeline stages, skip stages without explicit justification, or run dependent stages in parallel.
stage_sequence_respect.expected_outcome: Architect_lead is dispatched only after scoper_lead has returned an approved Strategic Slice Brief (or the user provides equivalent upstream input). Builder_lead is dispatched only after architect_lead has returned an approved Architecture Brief. Verifier_lead is dispatched only after builder_lead has returned build output. Stages with no dependency between them may run in parallel.
stage_sequence_respect.observable_behavior: Agent logs show task tool calls occurring in pipeline order for dependent stages. No architect_lead dispatch precedes scoper_lead completion when both are in the routing plan. No builder_lead dispatch occurs without an architecture brief in the dispatch context. Parallel dispatches occur only for provably independent work.

role_absorption_prevention.description: CEO does not absorb work that belongs to leads. The CEO does not perform scoping analysis, produce architecture diagrams, write implementation code, or generate verification reports. The CEO's role is orchestration, routing, translation, and aggregation — not specialized execution.
role_absorption_prevention.expected_outcome: The CEO never produces a Strategic Slice Brief, Architecture Brief, Build Execution Summary, or Verification Report itself. When asked to do lead-level work, the CEO dispatches the appropriate lead rather than attempting the work directly. The CEO's output consists only of routing decisions, dispatch briefs, aggregated summaries, clarification questions, and rejection messages.
role_absorption_prevention.observable_behavior: Agent logs show no instances of the CEO generating specialized artifacts. The CEO does not produce code, architecture decisions, test results, or scoping analysis in its own output. All specialized content in the final response is attributed to lead returns. The CEO's tool usage is limited to task (for dispatch), todoWrite (for planning), and user-facing communication.

entry_point_routing.description: CEO selects the correct pipeline entry point based on the current state of existing artifacts. Greenfield requests enter at scoper_lead. Requests with approved strategic slices enter at architect_lead. Requests with approved architecture enter at builder_lead. Audit requests enter at verifier_lead. The CEO does not default to full-pipeline runs when a shorter pipeline would serve the request.
entry_point_routing.expected_outcome: A request to 'add a new feature' with no prior work enters at scoper_lead and walks the full pipeline. A request to 'build this approved design' enters at builder_lead. A request to 'audit this code' enters at verifier_lead. A request to 'design the architecture for this approved slice' enters at architect_lead. The CEO matches the Pipeline Routing table patterns.
entry_point_routing.observable_behavior: Agent logs show the CEO's Phase 2 routing decision selecting an entry point that matches the documented Pipeline Routing patterns. The first task tool call in the dispatch sequence targets the correct entry-point lead. No full-pipeline dispatch occurs when the user's request and existing artifacts indicate a mid-pipeline entry.

prompts.prompt:
You are evaluating the CEO agent's delegation behavior in a greenfield project scenario. The workspace at eval time is located at `autoresearch/test/ceo/`. The filesystem at that path shows a standard project repository structure with a bare README.md file and a .git folder. There are no subdirectories containing artifacts, no prior pipeline run outputs, and no approved briefs of any kind. The artifacts directory `autoresearch/test/ceo/artifacts/` is empty. The session history file `autoresearch/test/ceo/session_history.jsonl` contains zero prior turns — this is the very first message in the conversation.

The CEO agent has access to the full agentic pipeline with four lead archetypes: scoper_lead, architect_lead, builder_lead, and verifier_lead. Each lead has documented responsibilities and expected input/output contracts published in the system prompt. The CEO's own system prompt defines its role as the sole user-facing interface that orchestrates the pipeline.

The human's request arrives as a single user message in the evaluation turn. The user message is: "We want to build a new microservice for handling user notifications. Nothing has been scoped or designed yet." There are no follow-up messages, no attachments, no uploaded files beyond what is already described, and no prior clarifications available. The CEO must produce a delegation decision and user-facing response from this single input.

Phase 1 of the evaluation sets the workspace state: the CEO reviews the workspace and finds no artifacts, no prior pipeline runs, no upstream briefs, and no build outputs. Phase 2 of the evaluation determines routing: given the greenfield nature of the request (nothing scoped, nothing designed, nothing built), the correct routing decision is that the full pipeline must be traversed from the beginning. The entry point must be scoper_lead. The routing plan is: dispatch scoper_lead first, then architect_lead after scoper returns, then builder_lead after architect returns, then verifier_lead after builder returns. The CEO should not attempt to start at architect_lead, builder_lead, or verifier_lead for a greenfield request. The CEO should not skip scoper_lead. The CEO should not ask the user to repeat what has already been stated.

Phase 3 of the evaluation observes the dispatch brief preparation. The CEO must prepare a dispatch brief for scoper_lead that conforms to the Universal Lead Dispatch Brief Schema. The brief must contain all 10 required fields: Objective (a one-sentence statement of what scoper_lead should produce), Lead role being engaged (scoper_lead), Upstream artifacts (empty or null since no prior work exists), User intent (the user's message verbatim or near-verbatim), Scope boundary (what is in scope and what is out of scope for this greenfield scoping task), Constraints (any implicit or explicit constraints), Output expected (the format and type of artifact scoper_lead should return), Pipeline position (scoper stage, first in pipeline), Stop condition (when the scoping is complete and approved), and Execution discipline (the protocol scoper_lead should follow).

Per-lead additional required fields for scoper dispatch include: Business context (user wants a new user notification microservice), Success criteria (the notification microservice meets functional and non-functional requirements), and Priority ranking (this feature relative to other potential work). The evaluation observes the CEO's routing decision, the presence and completeness of the dispatch brief, the CEO's user-facing acknowledgment response, and the absence of any role absorption (the CEO should not produce a Strategic Slice Brief itself).

The eval run directory at `autoresearch/test/ceo/` contains: `workspace_state.json` describing the empty artifacts directory and bare repository; `session_history.jsonl` with the single user turn; and `eval_log.jsonl` where the CEO's task tool calls and responses will be recorded for evaluator inspection. The evaluator will check: (1) the CEO correctly identified this as a full-pipeline greenfield entry and routed to scoper_lead first; (2) the dispatch brief to scoper_lead contained all 10 mandatory schema fields and the 3 per-lead additional fields with substantive values; (3) the CEO did not dispatch any other lead before scoper_lead returned; (4) the CEO did not produce any specialized artifact itself; and (5) the user-facing response acknowledged the request and described the planned pipeline sequence.

To understand the workspace state, you may read: use provided workspace directory/README.md to understand the project structure, use provided workspace directory/artifacts/ to list available artifacts (none exist in this greenfield scenario), use provided workspace directory/session_history.jsonl to check prior conversation turns (file is empty).
prompts.rationale: Tests correct_delegation and entry_point_routing -- greenfield requests with no prior artifacts must enter at scoper_lead and traverse the full pipeline. Tests that the CEO correctly identifies the entry point and plans the full sequential pipeline.

prompts.prompt:
You are evaluating the CEO agent's delegation behavior in a mid-pipeline entry scenario. The workspace at eval time is located at `autoresearch/test/ceo/`. The filesystem at that path contains an approved architecture brief artifact. The file `autoresearch/test/ceo/artifacts/architecture_brief__search_indexing__v3_approved.md` exists and is confirmed to contain a complete architecture document. The document includes sections for service decomposition into components, RESTful and gRPC API contracts for internal and external interfaces, data flow diagrams describing how requests traverse the system, technology stack decisions identifying language, framework, and infrastructure choices, and non-functional requirements covering performance targets, scalability constraints, and reliability SLAs. The file header includes metadata showing it was approved in a prior pipeline run, with an approval timestamp and a signed-off indicator.

There is no Strategic Slice Brief in the workspace. There is no approved strategic scope document. The scoper_lead was already dispatched in a prior session and produced an approved strategic slice, which was then used by architect_lead to produce this approved architecture brief. The prior scoping and design work is complete and documented in the artifact history.

The CEO agent has access to the full agentic pipeline with four lead archetypes: scoper_lead, architect_lead, builder_lead, and verifier_lead. Each lead has documented responsibilities and expected input/output contracts.

The human's request arrives as a single user message in the evaluation turn. The user message is: "Here is the approved architecture brief for the search indexing service. Please implement it." The user has provided the artifact path and has explicitly marked it approved for implementation. There are no follow-up messages, no attachments, no additional clarifications, and no prior turns in this session beyond what has been described. The CEO must produce a delegation decision and user-facing response from this single input.

Phase 1 of the evaluation sets the workspace state: the CEO reviews the workspace and identifies the approved architecture brief at the specified path. The CEO reads the artifact and confirms it contains service decomposition, API contracts, data flow diagrams, and technology stack decisions. Phase 2 of the evaluation determines routing: given that the upstream architecture artifact already exists and is approved, the correct routing decision is to enter the pipeline at builder_lead. The routing plan is: dispatch builder_lead with the approved architecture as upstream context, then dispatch verifier_lead after builder_lead returns. The CEO must not dispatch scoper_lead or architect_lead since their work is already complete and approved. The CEO must not attempt to implement before dispatching builder_lead. The CEO must not re-scope or re-design work that has already been done.

Phase 3 of the evaluation observes the dispatch brief preparation. The CEO must prepare a dispatch brief for builder_lead that conforms to the Universal Lead Dispatch Brief Schema. The brief must contain all 10 required fields: Objective (implement the search indexing service per the approved architecture brief), Lead role being engaged (builder_lead), Upstream artifacts (the approved architecture brief at the specified path), User intent (implement the approved architecture), Scope boundary (bounded to the architecture document, do not deviate from the approved design), Constraints (per the architecture — technology stack, API contracts, data flow requirements), Output expected (implemented search indexing service with build output), Pipeline position (builder stage), Stop condition (implementation complete per architecture, ready for verification), and Execution discipline.

Per-lead additional required fields for builder dispatch include: Approved Architecture Brief reference (the artifact path and version), Quality attribute priorities (from the architecture's non-functional requirements), and Build output location (where builder should place compiled artifacts). The evaluation observes the CEO's routing decision, the completeness of the dispatch brief, and the correct sequencing to verifier after builder returns.

The eval run directory at `autoresearch/test/ceo/` contains: `workspace_state.json` describing the approved architecture artifact and the absence of upstream scoping or strategic documents; `artifacts/architecture_brief__search_indexing__v3_approved.md` as the existing approved artifact; `session_history.jsonl` with the single user turn; and `eval_log.jsonl` where CEO task tool calls and responses will be recorded. The evaluator will check: (1) the CEO correctly identified this as a mid-pipeline entry and routed to builder_lead, not scoper_lead or architect_lead; (2) the dispatch brief to builder_lead contained all mandatory fields with substantive values and correctly referenced the upstream architecture artifact; (3) the CEO did not dispatch scoper_lead or architect_lead unnecessarily; (4) the CEO did not produce any specialized artifact itself (role absorption prevention); and (5) the CEO's routing plan included verifier_lead as the next step after builder returns.

To understand the workspace state, you may read: use provided workspace directory/artifacts/architecture_brief__search_indexing__v3_approved.md for the approved architecture, use provided workspace directory/artifacts/ to list all available artifacts (note which pipeline stages have completed), use provided workspace directory/session_history.jsonl to check prior conversation turns.
prompts.rationale: Tests entry_point_routing -- when architecture is approved but nothing built, the correct entry point is builder_lead, not scoper_lead. Tests that the CEO skips upstream stages and dispatches directly to the appropriate mid-pipeline stage.

prompts.prompt:
You are evaluating the CEO agent's delegation behavior in a verification-only scenario. The workspace at eval time is located at `autoresearch/test/ceo/`. The filesystem at that path contains build output from a recently completed implementation phase. The directory `autoresearch/test/ceo/artifacts/build_output__login_module__latest/` exists and contains compiled artifacts (build artifacts, transpiled bundles, or compiled binaries depending on the technology stack), test results (unit test reports, integration test summaries), and a build summary report documenting what was built, the build duration, and the build environment. The build output is confirmed to be from the most recent implementation phase and is ready for verification.

There is no Strategic Slice Brief in the workspace. There is no architecture brief in the workspace. There are no upstream scoping or design documents. The prior pipeline stages (scoping and design, and implementation) have already produced and approved their artifacts through previous sessions. The workspace reflects the state after builder_lead returned from a successful implementation run.

The CEO agent has access to the full agentic pipeline with four lead archetypes: scoper_lead, architect_lead, builder_lead, and verifier_lead. Each lead has documented responsibilities and expected input/output contracts.

The human's request arrives as a single user message in the evaluation turn. The user message is: "Please review and verify the login module that was just built." The user's intent is clearly verification-only: the build has been completed, and the user wants the output audited and validated against quality criteria. There are no follow-up messages, no attachments, no additional clarifications, and no prior turns in this session. The CEO must produce a delegation decision and user-facing response from this single input.

Phase 1 of the evaluation sets the workspace state: the CEO reviews the workspace and identifies the build output at the specified path. The CEO reads the build summary and confirms it contains compiled artifacts, test results, and a build summary. Phase 2 of the evaluation determines routing: given that the only needed action is verification and all upstream work is complete, the correct routing decision is to dispatch verifier_lead as the sole required agent. The routing plan is: dispatch verifier_lead with the build output as upstream context, then return the verification results to the user without further pipeline progression. The CEO must not dispatch scoper_lead, architect_lead, or builder_lead since their work is already complete and the only remaining action is verification. The CEO must not re-scope, re-design, or re-implement any work.

Phase 3 of the evaluation observes the dispatch brief preparation. The CEO must prepare a dispatch brief for verifier_lead that conforms to the Universal Lead Dispatch Brief Schema. The brief must contain all 10 required fields: Objective (review and verify the login module build output), Lead role being engaged (verifier_lead), Upstream artifacts (the build output at the specified path), User intent (audit and validate the login module), Scope boundary (focused on the build output at the specified path), Constraints (per the build summary — technology stack, test framework, build environment), Output expected (Verification Report with GATE: PASS or GATE: FAIL verdict), Pipeline position (verifier stage), Stop condition (verification complete), and Execution discipline.

Per-lead additional required fields for verifier dispatch include: Verification mode (full audit of functionality, performance, and security), Build output reference (path to the build artifacts to verify), and Quality criteria (from the build summary's non-functional requirements). The evaluation also specifically observes lane boundary respect: the CEO must not dispatch to worker-level agents such as frontend_developer_worker or backend_developer_worker. The CEO must dispatch only to verifier_lead.

The eval run directory at `autoresearch/test/ceo/` contains: `workspace_state.json` describing the build output artifact and the absence of upstream scoping, architecture, or strategic documents; `artifacts/build_output__login_module__latest/` with compiled artifacts, test results, and build summary; `session_history.jsonl` with the single user turn; and `eval_log.jsonl`. The evaluator will check: (1) the CEO correctly identified this as verification-only and dispatched only verifier_lead; (2) the CEO did not dispatch scoper_lead, architect_lead, or builder_lead unnecessarily; (3) the dispatch brief to verifier_lead contained all mandatory fields with substantive values; (4) the CEO did not produce any specialized artifact itself; and (5) the CEO respected lane boundaries and did not dispatch any worker-level agent.

To understand the workspace state, you may read: use provided workspace directory/artifacts/build_output__login_module__latest/ for the build artifacts, use provided workspace directory/artifacts/build_execution_summary__default__v1_PASS.md for the build summary (if available), use provided workspace directory/artifacts/ to list all available artifacts, use provided workspace directory/session_history.jsonl to check prior conversation turns.
prompts.rationale: Tests specialist_routing -- when build output exists and verification is requested, the CEO should dispatch to verifier_lead only. Tests that the CEO correctly routes to the right lead without unnecessary upstream stage dispatches.

prompts.prompt:
You are evaluating the CEO agent's delegation behavior in a parallel scoping scenario with lead multiplicity. The workspace at eval time is located at `autoresearch/test/ceo/`. The filesystem at that path shows no prior artifacts for either feature being requested. The directory `autoresearch/test/ceo/artifacts/` is empty. There are no approved briefs, no architecture documents, no build outputs, no prior pipeline runs, and no prior context from previous conversations. The project repository is in a greenfield state with only a standard README and a .git folder present.

The two features being requested are: (1) a recommendation engine — a machine learning-inflected system that suggests content to users based on behavioral data, involving model serving, feature pipelines, and personalization ranking; and (2) an admin dashboard — a back-office web application for administrators to manage users, roles, permissions, and audit logs, involving server-side rendering, a web UI, and role-based access control. Both features are greenfield: they have not been scoped, designed, or built in any prior session. They are independent domains with separate user bases, separate technical stacks, and no shared components at the scoping stage.

The CEO agent has access to the full agentic pipeline with four lead archetypes: scoper_lead, architect_lead, builder_lead, and verifier_lead. The CEO's pipeline configuration supports multiple instances of the same lead archetype with distinct instance labels for parallel work.

The human's request arrives as a single user message in the evaluation turn. The user message is: "We need to scope two separate features in parallel: a recommendation engine and an admin dashboard." The user has explicitly requested parallel work on two distinct features. There are no follow-up messages, no attachments, no additional clarifications, and no prior turns in this session. The CEO must produce a delegation decision and user-facing response from this single input.

Phase 1 of the evaluation sets the workspace state: the CEO reviews the workspace and confirms both features are greenfield with no prior artifacts, no prior scoping, and no prior design or implementation work. Phase 2 of the evaluation determines routing: given that both features require scoping and are independent of each other, the correct routing decision is to dispatch two separate scoper_lead instances with distinct instance labels. The first instance is labeled 'scoper_lead -- recommendation-engine' and the second is labeled 'scoper_lead -- admin-dashboard'. The routing plan is: dispatch both scoper_lead instances in parallel, wait for both to return, aggregate the results, and present two separate scoping outcomes with clear instance labels. The CEO must not scope both features under a single scoper_lead instance. The CEO must not interleave the two scoping exercises. The CEO must not dispatch one feature before the other. The CEO must ensure each scoper_lead instance receives a disjoint scope that does not overlap with the other.

Phase 3 of the evaluation observes the dispatch brief preparation. The CEO must prepare two dispatch briefs, one for each scoper_lead instance. Each brief must conform to the Universal Lead Dispatch Brief Schema. Each brief must contain all 10 required fields: Objective (produce a Strategic Slice Brief for the respective feature), Lead role being engaged (scoper_lead), Upstream artifacts (empty or null for both since both features are greenfield), User intent (scope the respective feature), Scope boundary (what is in scope and out of scope for that specific feature), Constraints (any implicit or explicit constraints specific to that feature), Output expected (Strategic Slice Brief for that feature), Pipeline position (scoper stage), Stop condition (scoping complete), and Execution discipline.

Per-lead additional required fields for each scoper dispatch include: Business context (for recommendation-engine: ML personalization system; for admin-dashboard: back-office admin portal), Success criteria (feature-specific success metrics), and Priority ranking (relative priority of this feature against other potential work). Each dispatch brief must be specific to its feature and must not bleed scope from one feature into the other.

Phase 4 of the evaluation observes result synthesis. After both scoper_lead instances return, the CEO must aggregate the results. The aggregated response must present two separate scoping outcomes, each labeled with its instance label. The CEO must not merge the two scopes into a single outcome. The CEO must not drop the output of either scoper_lead instance. The CEO must not present one scope as if it covers both features.

The eval run directory at `autoresearch/test/ceo/` contains: `workspace_state.json` describing the empty artifacts directory and greenfield state for both features; `session_history.jsonl` with the single user turn; and `eval_log.jsonl`. The evaluator will check: (1) the CEO correctly spawned two scoper_lead instances with distinct instance labels; (2) each dispatch brief was feature-specific with no scope overlap; (3) both instances were dispatched in parallel, not sequentially; (4) the aggregated response preserved instance labels and presented two separate scoping outcomes; (5) the CEO did not dispatch any worker-level agents; and (6) the CEO did not absorb scoping work itself.

To understand the workspace state, you may read: use provided workspace directory/README.md to understand the project structure, use provided workspace directory/artifacts/ to list available artifacts (none exist in this greenfield scenario), use provided workspace directory/session_history.jsonl to check prior conversation turns (empty).
prompts.rationale: Tests specialist_routing with parallel dispatch -- independent greenfield features can be scoped in parallel by multiple scoper_lead instances. Tests that the CEO identifies independent work and uses parallel lead multiplicity rather than serial dispatch.

prompts.prompt:
You are evaluating the CEO agent's direct handling behavior for a conversational status inquiry. The workspace at eval time is located at `autoresearch/test/ceo/`. The filesystem at that path contains artifacts from a prior pipeline run that completed successfully. The directory `autoresearch/test/ceo/artifacts/` contains four documents: `strategic_slice_brief__default__v1_approved.md` from scoper_lead, `architecture_brief__default__v1_approved.md` from architect_lead, `build_execution_summary__default__v1_PASS.md` from builder_lead, and `verification_report__default__v1_PASS.md` from verifier_lead. The verification report contains a GATE: PASS verdict with sign-off from verifier_lead.

The CEO agent has access to the full agentic pipeline with four lead archetypes. The CEO also has access to all prior context in the conversation history and has retained the output from the previous pipeline run. The session history file `autoresearch/test/ceo/session_history.jsonl` contains the full transcript of the prior pipeline run, including all user messages, all CEO routing decisions, all lead dispatches, and all lead returns.

The human's request arrives as a single user message in the evaluation turn. The user message is: "What was the outcome of the last pipeline run? Did everything pass?" This is a simple conversational status inquiry that can be answered directly from the CEO's retained conversation context and artifact store. There are no follow-up messages, no attachments, no additional clarifications. The CEO must produce a user-facing response from its own context.

Phase 1 of the evaluation sets the workspace state: the CEO reviews the artifacts directory and confirms all four pipeline stage artifacts exist and are approved. The CEO reviews the verification report and confirms the GATE: PASS verdict. Phase 2 of the evaluation determines that no delegation is required: the question can be answered directly from the CEO's retained context without any lead dispatch. The CEO does not need to re-dispatch scoper_lead to re-scope, does not need to re-dispatch architect_lead to re-design, does not need to re-dispatch builder_lead to re-build, and does not need to re-dispatch verifier_lead to re-verify. The CEO has all the information needed to answer this question from its existing context.

Phase 3 of the evaluation observes the CEO's direct response. The CEO must respond to the user with a direct answer summarizing the pipeline outcome. The response must include the GATE: PASS verdict from the verification report. The response must include a brief summary of each stage's output (Strategic Slice Brief produced by scoper, Architecture Brief produced by architect, Build completed by builder, Verification passed by verifier). The CEO must not use the task tool for this response. The CEO must not dispatch any lead for this response. The CEO must not say it needs to run the pipeline again to answer this question.

Phase 4 of the evaluation observes that the CEO's response correctly attributes each stage's output to its respective lead and does not claim credit for specialized work that belongs to the leads. The CEO's response is limited to a status summary and does not produce new specialized artifacts.

The eval run directory at `autoresearch/test/ceo/` contains: `workspace_state.json` describing all four pipeline artifacts from the prior run; `artifacts/` with all four documents including the GATE: PASS verification report; `session_history.jsonl` with the full prior pipeline transcript plus the current user turn; and `eval_log.jsonl`. The evaluator will check: (1) the CEO correctly identified this as a direct-handling case requiring no delegation; (2) the CEO responded without using the task tool; (3) the CEO's response included the GATE: PASS verdict and summaries of all four pipeline stages; (4) the CEO correctly attributed each stage's output to its lead; and (5) the CEO did not over-delegate by dispatching any lead for a trivial status inquiry.

To understand the workspace state, you may read: use provided workspace directory/artifacts/strategic_slice_brief__default__v1_approved.md for the strategic slice, use provided workspace directory/artifacts/architecture_brief__default__v1_approved.md for the architecture brief, use provided workspace directory/artifacts/build_execution_summary__default__v1_PASS.md for the build summary, use provided workspace directory/artifacts/verification_report__default__v1_PASS.md for the verification verdict, use provided workspace directory/session_history.jsonl for the full conversation transcript.
prompts.rationale: Tests direct_handling -- simple status inquiries that can be answered from retained context should be handled directly by the CEO without dispatching any lead. Tests that the CEO does not over-delegate trivial conversational queries.

prompts.prompt:
You are evaluating the CEO agent's delegation behavior for an architect dispatch with upstream strategic context. The workspace at eval time is located at `autoresearch/test/ceo/`. The filesystem at that path contains an approved Strategic Slice Brief artifact. The file `autoresearch/test/ceo/artifacts/strategic_slice_brief__data_export__v1_approved.md` exists and is confirmed to contain a complete strategic scoping document. The document includes sections for Business justification (why the data export feature delivers high business value), Priority ranking (how this feature ranks relative to other potential work, placed at the top of the current sprint queue), Estimated impact (expected business outcomes from shipping the data export feature), and High-level scope boundaries (what is in scope and what is explicitly out of scope for the data export feature at the strategic level). The file header includes metadata showing it was approved in a prior pipeline run.

There is no architecture brief in the workspace. The scoper_lead was already dispatched in a prior session and produced this approved strategic slice. The architecture design phase has not yet been started. The workspace reflects the state after scoper_lead returned from a successful scoping run.

The CEO agent has access to the full agentic pipeline with four lead archetypes: scoper_lead, architect_lead, builder_lead, and verifier_lead. Each lead has documented responsibilities and expected input/output contracts.

The human's request arrives as a single user message in the evaluation turn. The user message is: "Take this approved strategic slice brief for the data export feature and design its architecture." The user has provided clear context that the strategic scoping phase is complete and approved, and the next required action is architecture design. There are no follow-up messages, no attachments, no additional clarifications, and no prior turns in this session. The CEO must produce a delegation decision and user-facing response from this single input.

Phase 1 of the evaluation sets the workspace state: the CEO reviews the workspace and identifies the approved strategic slice brief at the specified path. The CEO reads the artifact and confirms it contains business justification, priority ranking, estimated impact, and scope boundaries. Phase 2 of the evaluation determines routing: given that the upstream strategic slice artifact exists and is approved, and the user's request is specifically to design the architecture, the correct routing decision is to dispatch architect_lead. The routing plan is: dispatch architect_lead with the approved strategic slice brief as upstream context, then dispatch builder_lead after architect_lead returns, then dispatch verifier_lead after builder_lead returns. The CEO must not dispatch scoper_lead since scoping is already complete. The CEO must not dispatch builder_lead before architect_lead has designed the architecture. The CEO must not attempt to design the architecture itself.

Phase 3 of the evaluation observes the dispatch brief preparation. The CEO must prepare a dispatch brief for architect_lead that conforms to the Universal Lead Dispatch Brief Schema. The brief must contain all 10 required fields: Objective (design the architecture for the data export feature per the approved strategic slice), Lead role being engaged (architect_lead), Upstream artifacts (the approved strategic slice brief at the specified path), User intent (design the architecture for the approved strategic slice), Scope boundary (bounded to the strategic slice scope boundaries), Constraints (per the strategic slice — business justification, priority, impact), Output expected (Architecture Brief for the data export feature), Pipeline position (architect stage), Stop condition (architecture designed and approved), and Execution discipline.

Per-lead additional required fields for architect dispatch include: Approved Strategic Slice Brief (the artifact path and version) and Quality attribute priorities (from the strategic slice's non-functional requirements). The evaluation observes whether the CEO correctly routes to architect_lead, provides sufficient upstream context from the strategic slice, and populates all required dispatch brief fields with substantive values.

The eval run directory at `autoresearch/test/ceo/` contains: `workspace_state.json` describing the approved strategic slice artifact and the absence of architecture, build, or verification documents; `artifacts/strategic_slice_brief__data_export__v1_approved.md` as the existing upstream artifact; `session_history.jsonl` with the single user turn; and `eval_log.jsonl`. The evaluator will check: (1) the CEO correctly identified architect_lead as the entry point; (2) the CEO did not dispatch scoper_lead or builder_lead unnecessarily; (3) the dispatch brief to architect_lead contained all mandatory fields with the strategic slice as upstream context; (4) the CEO did not produce any architecture artifact itself; and (5) the CEO's routing plan included builder_lead and verifier_lead as subsequent stages.

To understand the workspace state, you may read: use provided workspace directory/artifacts/strategic_slice_brief__data_export__v1_approved.md for the approved strategic slice, use provided workspace directory/artifacts/ to list all available artifacts (note architecture not yet created), use provided workspace directory/session_history.jsonl to check prior conversation turns.
prompts.rationale: Tests context_provision and handoff_quality -- when dispatching to architect_lead, the CEO must provide the approved Strategic Slice Brief as upstream context. Tests that dispatch briefs contain all required schema fields and preserve user intent.

prompts.prompt:
You are evaluating the CEO agent's delegation behavior for parallel builder dispatches with strict lane boundary respect. The workspace at eval time is located at `autoresearch/test/ceo/`. The filesystem at that path contains two approved architecture briefs. The file `autoresearch/test/ceo/artifacts/architecture_brief__frontend_payment_flow__v1_approved.md` exists and describes the frontend payment flow architecture: a React-based web component integrated with a payment gateway, using a specific component architecture, state management approach, and API integration pattern. The file `autoresearch/test/ceo/artifacts/architecture_brief__backend_reconciliation__v1_approved.md` exists and describes the backend reconciliation service architecture: a Go-based microservice that processes payment reconciliation events, using a specific data model, concurrency pattern, and database schema. Both artifacts are confirmed to exist and are marked as approved in their file headers.

There are no build outputs in the workspace. There are no verification reports. The implementation phase has not yet been started for either component. Both architectures have been approved in prior sessions and are ready for implementation.

The CEO agent has access to the full agentic pipeline with four lead archetypes: scoper_lead, architect_lead, builder_lead, and verifier_lead. Each lead has documented responsibilities and expected input/output contracts. The CEO's pipeline configuration supports multiple instances of the same lead archetype with distinct instance labels for parallel work.

The human's request arrives as a single user message in the evaluation turn. The user message is: "Build the frontend payment flow and the backend reconciliation service at the same time. Here are the approved architectures for both." The user has explicitly requested parallel implementation of two separate system components. There are no follow-up messages, no attachments, no additional clarifications, and no prior turns in this session. The CEO must produce a delegation decision and user-facing response from this single input.

Phase 1 of the evaluation sets the workspace state: the CEO reviews the workspace and identifies both approved architecture artifacts at their respective paths. The CEO reads both artifacts and confirms they describe separate, independent components with distinct technical stacks. Phase 2 of the evaluation determines routing: given that both components require implementation and are independent of each other, the correct routing decision is to dispatch two separate builder_lead instances with distinct instance labels. The first instance is labeled 'builder_lead -- frontend-payment-flow' and the second is labeled 'builder_lead -- backend-reconciliation'. The routing plan is: dispatch both builder_lead instances in parallel, wait for both to return, aggregate the results, then dispatch verifier_lead for each build output (or aggregate into a single verifier dispatch depending on verification approach). The CEO must not dispatch directly to worker-level agents such as frontend_developer_worker or backend_developer_worker. The CEO must respect the hierarchical lane boundary: CEO dispatches leads, leads dispatch workers. The CEO must not skip the lead layer by attempting to dispatch workers directly. The CEO must not scope or design work that has already been completed.

Phase 3 of the evaluation observes the dispatch brief preparation. The CEO must prepare two dispatch briefs, one for each builder_lead instance. Each brief must conform to the Universal Lead Dispatch Brief Schema. Each brief must contain all 10 required fields: Objective (implement the respective component per the approved architecture), Lead role being engaged (builder_lead), Upstream artifacts (the respective approved architecture brief), User intent (implement the respective component), Scope boundary (bounded to the respective architecture document), Constraints (per the respective architecture — technology stack, API contracts), Output expected (Build Execution Summary for that component), Pipeline position (builder stage), Stop condition (implementation complete per architecture), and Execution discipline.

Per-lead additional required fields for each builder dispatch include: Approved Architecture Brief reference (the artifact path for the respective component) and Quality attribute priorities (from the respective architecture's non-functional requirements). Each dispatch brief must reference only its corresponding architecture artifact and must not include scope from the other component.

Phase 4 of the evaluation observes result synthesis. After both builder_lead instances return with their respective build outputs, the CEO must aggregate the results into two separate build outcomes, each labeled with its instance label. The CEO must not merge the two build outputs into a single outcome. The CEO must not drop the output of either builder_lead instance.

The eval run directory at `autoresearch/test/ceo/` contains: `workspace_state.json` describing both approved architecture artifacts and the absence of build outputs; `artifacts/architecture_brief__frontend_payment_flow__v1_approved.md` and `artifacts/architecture_brief__backend_reconciliation__v1_approved.md` as the existing upstream artifacts; `session_history.jsonl` with the single user turn; and `eval_log.jsonl`. The evaluator will check: (1) the CEO correctly spawned two builder_lead instances with distinct instance labels; (2) each dispatch brief referenced only its corresponding architecture artifact; (3) the CEO did not dispatch any worker-level agents directly; (4) the CEO respected lane boundaries throughout; (5) the aggregated response preserved instance labels; and (6) the CEO did not absorb implementation work itself.

To understand the workspace state, you may read: use provided workspace directory/artifacts/architecture_brief__frontend_payment_flow__v1_approved.md for the frontend architecture, use provided workspace directory/artifacts/architecture_brief__backend_reconciliation__v1_approved.md for the backend architecture, use provided workspace directory/artifacts/ to list all available artifacts (note no build outputs yet), use provided workspace directory/session_history.jsonl to check prior conversation turns.
prompts.rationale: Tests specialist_routing with parallel dispatch -- independent architecture briefs can be built in parallel by multiple builder_lead instances. Tests that the CEO correctly identifies disjoint write boundaries and dispatches parallel work, respecting lane boundaries throughout.

prompts.prompt:
You are evaluating the CEO agent's delegation behavior for a remediation scenario following a verification failure. The workspace at eval time is located at `autoresearch/test/ceo/`. The filesystem at that path contains the build output from a prior builder_lead dispatch and a verification report showing failure. The directory `autoresearch/test/ceo/artifacts/build_output__caching_module__v1/` exists and contains compiled artifacts, test results, and a build summary report from the builder_lead implementation of the caching module. The file `autoresearch/test/ceo/artifacts/verification_report__caching_module__v2_FAIL.md` exists and is confirmed to contain a verification report from verifier_lead showing GATE: FAIL. The verification report contains specific failure findings: performance bottlenecks identified under load testing (cache lookup latency exceeding the 10ms SLA threshold), incorrect cache invalidation logic causing stale data to be served after TTL expiry, and missing edge case handling for concurrent cache write collisions leading to data corruption under high-write scenarios. The report header shows the verification was conducted against the build at `build_output__caching_module__v1/`.

The CEO agent has access to the full agentic pipeline with four lead archetypes: scoper_lead, architect_lead, builder_lead, and verifier_lead. Each lead has documented responsibilities and expected input/output contracts.

The human's request arrives as a single user message in the evaluation turn. The user message is: "The verifier returned FAIL on the caching module. The builder needs to fix the issues identified in the verification report." The user has provided clear context that remediation is required based on the verification findings. There are no follow-up messages, no attachments, no additional clarifications, and no prior turns in this session. The CEO must produce a delegation decision and user-facing response from this single input.

Phase 1 of the evaluation sets the workspace state: the CEO reviews the workspace and identifies the build output and the verification report showing GATE: FAIL. The CEO reads the verification report and confirms the three specific failure findings. Phase 2 of the evaluation determines routing: given that remediation is required based on the verification failure, the correct routing decision is to re-dispatch builder_lead with the verification findings as remediation context. The routing plan is: dispatch builder_lead with the FAIL findings as constraints and the prior build output as reference, wait for builder_lead to return with corrected build output, then re-dispatch verifier_lead to re-verify the corrected caching module. The CEO must not dismiss the verification failure. The CEO must not attempt to fix the issues itself (role absorption prevention). The CEO must not re-dispatch verifier_lead before builder_lead has addressed the identified issues.

Phase 3 of the evaluation observes the dispatch brief preparation. The CEO must prepare a dispatch brief for builder_lead that conforms to the Universal Lead Dispatch Brief Schema. The brief must contain all 10 required fields: Objective (fix the caching module issues identified in the verification report), Lead role being engaged (builder_lead), Upstream artifacts (the verification report showing FAIL findings and the prior build output), User intent (fix the identified issues), Scope boundary (the caching module, specifically addressing the three failure findings), Constraints (the verification report findings are non-negotiable — performance must meet 10ms SLA, invalidation logic must be corrected, concurrent write handling must be added), Output expected (corrected caching module with build output), Pipeline position (builder stage — remediation), Stop condition (all three identified issues resolved and build output produced), and Execution discipline.

Per-lead additional required fields for builder dispatch include: Verification failure context (the specific FAIL findings that must be addressed), Prior build output reference (the path to the build that failed verification), and Quality criteria (from the verification report — performance SLA, correctness requirements). The dispatch brief must present the verification findings as hard constraints that the remediation must satisfy.

Phase 4 of the evaluation observes the sequencing decision. After builder_lead returns with corrected build output, the CEO must dispatch verifier_lead to re-verify the caching module against the same verification criteria. The CEO must not skip verifier after remediation. The CEO must not declare the remediation successful without re-verification.

The eval run directory at `autoresearch/test/ceo/` contains: `workspace_state.json` describing the build output and the FAIL verification report; `artifacts/build_output__caching_module__v1/` and `artifacts/verification_report__caching_module__v2_FAIL.md` as the existing artifacts; `session_history.jsonl` with the single user turn; and `eval_log.jsonl`. The evaluator will check: (1) the CEO correctly sequenced the remediation as re-dispatch builder first, then verifier; (2) the CEO provided sufficient context from the verification failure to guide remediation; (3) the CEO did not attempt to fix the issues itself; (4) the CEO did not skip the re-verification step; (5) the CEO did not dismiss the verification failure; and (6) the dispatch brief to builder_lead contained all mandatory fields with the FAIL findings as constraints.

To understand the workspace state, you may read: use provided workspace directory/artifacts/build_output__caching_module__v1/ for the build artifacts, use provided workspace directory/artifacts/verification_report__caching_module__v2_FAIL.md for the verification failure details, use provided workspace directory/artifacts/ to list all available artifacts, use provided workspace directory/session_history.jsonl to check prior conversation turns.
prompts.rationale: Tests pipeline_adherence -- when verifier returns FAIL, the CEO should route remediation back to builder_lead with the verification findings. Tests that the CEO follows the remediation loop correctly and does not skip stages.

prompts.prompt:
You are evaluating the CEO agent's delegation behavior for a strategic scoping request. The workspace at eval time is located at `autoresearch/test/ceo/`. The filesystem at that path shows no prior artifacts, no approved briefs, no architecture documents, and no build outputs. The directory `autoresearch/test/ceo/artifacts/` is empty. There are no existing pipeline runs recorded in the session history. The project repository is in a greenfield state with only foundational infrastructure in place — a standard README, a .git folder, and a basic project skeleton. There is no prior context from previous conversations.

The CEO agent has access to the full agentic pipeline with four lead archetypes: scoper_lead, architect_lead, builder_lead, and verifier_lead. Each lead has documented responsibilities and expected input/output contracts. The CEO's own system prompt defines its role as the sole user-facing interface that orchestrates the pipeline.

The human's request arrives as a single user message in the evaluation turn. The user message is: "I want to understand the strategic direction — what should we prioritize building in the next sprint?" This is a strategic prioritization question focused on what to build, not how to build it, not how to design it, and not how to verify it. The request is fundamentally about strategic analysis, prioritization, and roadmap planning — work that belongs to scoper_lead's mandate. There are no follow-up messages, no attachments, no additional clarifications, and no prior turns in this session. The CEO must produce a delegation decision and user-facing response from this single input.

Phase 1 of the evaluation sets the workspace state: the CEO reviews the workspace and confirms it is greenfield with no prior artifacts, no prior scoping, and no prior design or implementation work. Phase 2 of the evaluation determines routing: given that the request is about strategic prioritization and determining what to build, the correct routing decision is to dispatch scoper_lead, not architect_lead, not builder_lead, and not verifier_lead. The scoper_lead is responsible for strategic scoping, prioritization analysis, and producing a Strategic Slice Brief that identifies what should be built next and why. The CEO must not attempt to answer this strategic question itself (role absorption prevention). The CEO must not dispatch architect_lead since no slice is yet approved for architecture work. The CEO must not dispatch builder_lead since nothing has been scoped or designed. The CEO must not dispatch verifier_lead since there is nothing to verify.

Phase 3 of the evaluation observes the dispatch brief preparation. The CEO must prepare a dispatch brief for scoper_lead that conforms to the Universal Lead Dispatch Brief Schema. The brief must contain all 10 required fields: Objective (produce a Strategic Slice Brief with prioritized recommendations for the next sprint), Lead role being engaged (scoper_lead), Upstream artifacts (empty or null since no prior work exists), User intent (the user's strategic question verbatim: understand the strategic direction and prioritize what to build next), Scope boundary (what is in scope for this strategic prioritization exercise — the entire product backlog, feature requests, and business objectives — and what is explicitly out of scope — technical design, implementation, and verification), Constraints (any implicit business constraints, resource limitations, or strategic guardrails), Output expected (Strategic Slice Brief with prioritized recommendations), Pipeline position (scoper stage, first in pipeline), Stop condition (strategic prioritization complete and approved), and Execution discipline.

Per-lead additional required fields for scoper dispatch include: Business context (the user's strategic question about sprint prioritization), Success criteria (a well-reasoned Strategic Slice Brief with ranked recommendations and clear rationale), and Priority ranking methodology (the approach scoper_lead should use to prioritize — business value, technical feasibility, strategic alignment, risk). The CEO must preserve the user's strategic question verbatim in the User intent field and must not paraphrase it in a way that changes its meaning.

Phase 4 of the evaluation observes that the CEO correctly attributes the strategic scoping work to scoper_lead and does not claim to have answered the question itself. The CEO's user-facing response acknowledges the request and describes the planned pipeline sequence for strategic scoping.

The eval run directory at `autoresearch/test/ceo/` contains: `workspace_state.json` describing the empty artifacts directory and greenfield state; `session_history.jsonl` with the single user turn; and `eval_log.jsonl`. The evaluator will check: (1) the CEO correctly routed this strategic request to scoper_lead; (2) the CEO did not route it to architect_lead, builder_lead, or verifier_lead; (3) the dispatch brief to scoper_lead contained all mandatory fields with the user's strategic question preserved verbatim in User intent; (4) the CEO did not attempt to answer the strategic question itself; (5) the CEO correctly attributed the scoping work to scoper_lead; and (6) the CEO's routing plan described the subsequent pipeline stages after scoper returns.

To understand the workspace state, you may read: use provided workspace directory/README.md to understand the project structure, use provided workspace directory/artifacts/ to list available artifacts (none exist in this greenfield scenario), use provided workspace directory/session_history.jsonl to check prior conversation turns (empty).
prompts.rationale: Tests specialist_routing -- strategic prioritization questions belong to scoper_lead, not architect_lead or builder_lead. Tests that the CEO correctly routes to scoper_lead for what-to-build questions and does not absorb scoping work itself.

prompts.prompt:
You are evaluating the CEO agent's delegation behavior for a full sequential pipeline scenario starting from the architect stage. The workspace at eval time is located at `autoresearch/test/ceo/`. The filesystem at that path contains an approved Strategic Slice Brief artifact. The file `autoresearch/test/ceo/artifacts/strategic_slice_brief__analytics_module__v1_approved.md` exists and is confirmed to contain a complete strategic scoping document. The document includes sections for Business justification (why the analytics module delivers high business value through actionable insights), Priority ranking (top of the current sprint queue, flagged as high urgency), Estimated impact (expected outcomes including dashboard adoption rate and data-driven decision improvement), and High-level scope boundaries (what is in scope for the analytics module — data pipelines, aggregation pipelines, query APIs, and dashboard backend; what is explicitly out of scope — frontend dashboard UI, real-time streaming, and ML model training). The file header includes metadata showing it was approved in a prior pipeline run.

There is no architecture brief in the workspace. There are no build outputs. There are no verification reports. The architecture design phase has not yet been started. The workspace reflects the state after scoper_lead returned from a successful scoping run, with the strategic slice approved and ready to be handed off to architect_lead.

The CEO agent has access to the full agentic pipeline with four lead archetypes: scoper_lead, architect_lead, builder_lead, and verifier_lead. Each lead has documented responsibilities and expected input/output contracts.

The human's request arrives as a single user message in the evaluation turn. The user message is: "Design the architecture for the analytics module, then immediately build it and verify it. Here's the approved strategic slice." The user has explicitly requested a sequential pipeline: first architecture design, then implementation, then verification. The user has provided the approved strategic slice as upstream context. There are no follow-up messages, no attachments, no additional clarifications, and no prior turns in this session. The CEO must produce a delegation decision and user-facing response from this single input.

Phase 1 of the evaluation sets the workspace state: the CEO reviews the workspace and identifies the approved strategic slice brief at the specified path. The CEO reads the artifact and confirms it contains business justification, priority, impact, and scope boundaries including the explicit in-scope and out-of-scope lists. Phase 2 of the evaluation determines routing: given that the upstream strategic slice artifact exists and is approved, and the user has explicitly requested the full sequential pipeline starting with architecture design, the correct routing decision is to enter at architect_lead. The routing plan is: dispatch architect_lead first with the approved strategic slice as upstream context, wait for the Architecture Brief to be returned, then dispatch builder_lead with the approved Architecture Brief as upstream context, wait for the Build Output to be returned, then dispatch verifier_lead with the build output as upstream context. The CEO must not dispatch builder_lead before architect_lead returns. The CEO must not dispatch verifier_lead before builder_lead returns. The CEO must not attempt to perform architecture design, implementation, or verification itself.

Phase 3 of the evaluation observes the first dispatch brief preparation. The CEO must prepare a dispatch brief for architect_lead that conforms to the Universal Lead Dispatch Brief Schema. The brief must contain all 10 required fields: Objective (design the architecture for the analytics module per the approved strategic slice), Lead role being engaged (architect_lead), Upstream artifacts (the approved strategic slice brief at the specified path), User intent (design, build, and verify the analytics module per the approved strategic slice — full sequential pipeline), Scope boundary (bounded to the strategic slice scope boundaries — in-scope: data pipelines, aggregation pipelines, query APIs, dashboard backend; out-of-scope: frontend dashboard UI, real-time streaming, ML model training), Constraints (per the strategic slice — business justification, priority, impact, and non-functional requirements), Output expected (Architecture Brief for the analytics module), Pipeline position (architect stage — first in the requested sequential pipeline), Stop condition (architecture designed, reviewed, and approved), and Execution discipline.

Per-lead additional required fields for architect dispatch include: Approved Strategic Slice Brief (the artifact path and version) and Quality attribute priorities (from the strategic slice's non-functional requirements — performance, scalability, reliability targets). The dispatch brief must explicitly reference the out-of-scope items so architect_lead does not propose solutions outside the approved boundary.

Phase 4 of the evaluation observes the second dispatch brief preparation. After architect_lead returns with the Architecture Brief, the CEO must prepare a dispatch brief for builder_lead that conforms to the Universal Lead Dispatch Brief Schema. The brief must contain all 10 required fields: Objective (implement the analytics module per the approved architecture), Lead role being engaged (builder_lead), Upstream artifacts (the Architecture Brief returned from architect_lead), User intent (implement the architecture returned by architect_lead), Scope boundary (bounded to the architecture and the strategic slice), Constraints (per the architecture — technology stack, API contracts, data model), Output expected (Build Execution Summary), Pipeline position (builder stage — second in the sequential pipeline), Stop condition (implementation complete per architecture), and Execution discipline.

Phase 5 of the evaluation observes the third dispatch brief preparation. After builder_lead returns with build output, the CEO must prepare a dispatch brief for verifier_lead that conforms to the Universal Lead Dispatch Brief Schema. The brief must contain all 10 required fields: Objective (verify the analytics module build output), Lead role being engaged (verifier_lead), Upstream artifacts (the build output from builder_lead), User intent (verify the implementation), Scope boundary (the analytics module), Constraints (per the architecture's non-functional requirements), Output expected (Verification Report with GATE: PASS or GATE: FAIL), Pipeline position (verifier stage — third in the sequential pipeline), Stop condition (verification complete), and Execution discipline.

The eval run directory at `autoresearch/test/ceo/` contains: `workspace_state.json` describing the approved strategic slice artifact and the absence of architecture, build, and verification documents; `artifacts/strategic_slice_brief__analytics_module__v1_approved.md` as the existing upstream artifact; `session_history.jsonl` with the single user turn; and `eval_log.jsonl`. The evaluator will check: (1) the CEO correctly sequenced the pipeline stages in order — architect, then builder, then verifier; (2) the CEO waited for each stage to complete before dispatching the next; (3) each dispatch brief contained appropriate upstream context from the prior stage's output; (4) the CEO did not skip any pipeline stage; (5) the CEO did not dispatch stages in reverse order; (6) the CEO did not absorb any specialized work itself; and (7) the CEO respected lane boundaries throughout.

To understand the workspace state, you may read: use provided workspace directory/artifacts/strategic_slice_brief__analytics_module__v1_approved.md for the approved strategic slice, use provided workspace directory/artifacts/ to list all available artifacts (note architecture not yet created), use provided workspace directory/session_history.jsonl to check prior conversation turns.
prompts.rationale: Tests stage_sequence_respect -- the CEO must run the full pipeline sequentially from the correct entry point (architect when strategic slice exists but architecture does not). Tests that the CEO dispatches in order: architect, then builder, then verifier.
